title: Day 27 Post-Mencho: CJNG Retains National Footprint; García Harfuch Warns Organization Remains Dangerous
detail: Day 27 since El Mencho's killing at Tapalpa, Jalisco (February 22, 2026). Security Secretary Omar García Harfuch publicly stated that 'el CJNG es una organización criminal que opera en todo el país' — acknowledging the cartel retains a full national operational footprint despite the loss of its founder and multiple senior commanders. This assessment is consistent with CJNG's franchise model, which was designed to survive leadership decapitation. Mexico's post-Tapalpa pressure campaign continues: 37+ high-value targets arrested or killed since February 22. O3 (Juan Carlos Valencia González) remains the reported successor but consolidation is still incomplete with El Jardinero controlling key regional plazas. International dimensions of the CJNG threat continue to expand: DOJ proceedings documented an international weapons-trafficking network — involving a Bulgarian intermediary — that sought to supply CJNG with military-grade equipment including missiles and explosives, highlighting the cartel's ongoing efforts to upgrade its paramilitary capacity during the succession period.

title: DOJ: International Network Including Bulgarian Trafficker Sought to Supply CJNG with Missiles and Military Explosives
detail: U.S. Department of Justice announced criminal proceedings against a Bulgarian national who allegedly coordinated an international weapons-trafficking network targeting CJNG as a client. The network sought to supply CJNG with high-powered military equipment including missiles and explosives — a significant escalation beyond the conventional firearms and RPGs previously documented in CJNG's arsenal. The use of forged documents was central to the scheme. The case underscores that CJNG is actively seeking to upgrade its military capability during the succession crisis — consistent with Grupo Élite's paramilitary orientation under O3's command. CJNG's prior weapons procurement has included Russian RPG-27 launchers (used in the 2015 helicopter shootdown), .50-caliber rifles, and commercially modified armored vehicles. A successful missile acquisition would represent a qualitative escalation of CJNG's anti-state capability. DEA and ATF are investigating U.S. links in the supply chain.

title: Operativo Enjambre: Five Chavinda, Michoacán Municipal Officials Arrested for CJNG Ties
detail: Five municipal government officials from Chavinda, Michoacán — reportedly connected to the Movimiento Ciudadano political party — were arrested on suspicion of nexos (links) to CJNG. During the Operativo Enjambre raids, authorities seized a tracked vehicle, conventional firearms, narcotics, and items bearing CJNG insignia from locations associated with the suspects, as well as official municipal government documents. Among the materials seized: tactical leg armor, a retractable baton, and internal government files. The arrests illustrate CJNG's systematic penetration of local political and administrative structures in Michoacán — a state it has contested with Los Viagras and La Nueva Familia Michoacana. Michoacán's importance to CJNG is economic: control of the Port of Lázaro Cárdenas and Tierra Caliente's avocado and lime extortion rackets provide hundreds of millions in annual revenue. The infiltration of municipal offices enables CJNG to pre-empt law enforcement operations, manage vehicle movement, and protect logistics corridors. The Chavinda arrests are part of a wider pattern of post-Mencho institutional cleanup operations.

title: FGR Acknowledges Evidence Contamination at Properties Where El Mencho's Narco Payroll Was Discovered
detail: The Mexican Attorney General's Office (FGR) publicly justified why it was unable to properly safeguard seized properties where documentary evidence of El Mencho's financial operations — including a narconómina (cartel payroll ledger) — was discovered following the February 22 operation. Officials stated that 'no había condiciones para resguardar los inmuebles' (there were no conditions to secure the properties), raising concerns about evidence integrity in the case against CJNG's remaining senior leadership. The discovery of a narconómina is significant: such documents typically contain coded identities of cartel members, corrupt officials on the payroll, and logistics contractors — intelligence gold for prosecutors and law enforcement. If the evidence was contaminated, key leads may be unusable in court. Opposition legislators and anti-corruption organizations have demanded a formal investigation into who had access to the properties between the operation and the FGR's arrival, and whether evidence was removed. The contamination controversy follows earlier reports that the scene of El Mencho's killing in Tapalpa had also been altered.

title: Guerrero Self-Defense Groups Rearmed Against La Nueva Familia Michoacana — CJNG Fractured Presence Noted
detail: An AP report filed from Guajes de Ayala, Guerrero, documents a 50-person community self-defense group that rearmed in October 2025 after La Nueva Familia Michoacana resumed territorial expansion into their mountain communities. The vigilantes — armed with AK-47s and AR-15s manufactured in Florida and South Carolina — monitor approximately 100 cartel gunmen using commercial drones. Notably, one vigilante previously belonged to CJNG, and another was photographed wearing apparel referencing El Mencho — suggesting CJNG's fractured post-Mencho presence is creating tactical voids in Guerrero that independent resistance movements and rival organizations are beginning to exploit. Two local gangs fighting La Nueva Familia Michoacana are reportedly allowing the community safe passage — illustrating the complex, shifting alignments that follow a major leadership vacuum. The Guerrero situation is a microcosm of the broader fragmentation dynamic: CJNG's reduced cohesion is creating tactical openings for rivals and self-defense groups in states previously under CJNG pressure.
